speaker: interviewer
text: --going to talk about crossing the border from Poland into Hungary.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. We were in Dolina and, as I mentioned, in Dolina we knew that we had some family there from our mother's side, the Drimmers. This family that Moshe stayed at, their son took us over to show us where the family, Drimmer, lives. We came in, only Tsvi and I came in there, and we introduced ourselves. And we said, we are Drimmers from-- they knew. They knew that they had family, that they had family in former Czechoslovakia, and they were very nice to us. We could feel right away that this is more than just-- we felt that we are among family. And this family, they had a beautiful pair of teenage boys, twins. They were about 14 or 15 years of age at that time. And the reason why I'm mentioning that, because it was very unusual, they were growing gray hair. They were very nice kids. And they told me, we only have room for one person. But they had in-laws that lived across the street from them that one of us can go over to-- they will take care of one of us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it turned out that my older brother, Tsvi stayed with them, and I was the one to go over to their in-laws. They were not relatives. But [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] in that house was a young girl, maybe a year younger than I was, and it turned out that she belonged to the same Betar organization, Zionist organization, that I belonged in Czechoslovakia before. So we felt a fraternal-- how should I say that-- closeness. And she was a secretary at the Judenrat. She worked in the Judenrat. The Judenrat had made kitchens that they would cook for refugees, Hungarian refugees, that filtered back in there. When I told her that I have two brothers, the one is here and the other one is with his wife and two children, that they were going to register in the Judenrat because they can get a meal there, she says, oh no, don't do that. Don't. You tell your brother not to go there. I said, well, why not? She says, I'll tell you why. Because the Germans come in every day, and they take people out for the dirtiest work. They demanded from the Judenrat to give them so many hands. And what the Judenrat usually does-- she was very open with it-- they usually, instead of sending local people, local Jews-- they send the Hungarian refugees. Of course, we notified our brother not to do it. And she says, don't worry about that. Little soup that you were going to get, whatever we will eat, you will eat with this. And it was really very, very-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] how should I say that? Very nice of her that-- what she did. She gave us the right answer. [SIGHS] Now we are 50 kilometers from home. 50 kilometers usually could be walked in about eight hours, 10 hours the most. But all of a sudden, we started hearing rumors coming in from people that went to the border and the border police caught them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took them in back to Hungary and then they loaded them up and shipped them out to Stanislawow, and you never heard from-- and in the meantime, some of the Hungarian border guards, they learned that this is a good opportunity to rob people. So they kill the people because they figured maybe they have maybe some jewelery, maybe some money. And it's very, very dangerous, absolutely impossible, to cross the border.

speaker: interviewee
text: And here we are in October, and this is the Carpathian. And it's not unusual in the Carpathian that in October, sometimes you get snow. In November, definitely you can get snow. And if you have snow, you have to cross a mountain in the snow. That's impossible. So what do we do? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Finally, we stay there-- I don't remember-- eight or 10 days in Dolina. And we decided, my brother and I, Tsvi and I decided, since we are alone-- Moshe with the children cannot go with us. We should go alone, try to cross that border, and maybe we can get in touch with our-- we knew that our parents are still in Torun. And we also knew that they had money because they gave us money when we parted. So we decided that we will take the chance, and when we will get over there, maybe we will be able to send somebody across for money and bring him back because we knew that the rabbi of Munkacs, the community, the Jewish community in Munkacs, managed to send somebody in to bring them over. And we knew that maybe for money this could be done. So one day we decided we are going next morning, and whatever we had money, we left our money-- Hungarian money-- we left it to my brother, Moshe but we left ourselves 40 pengos. We need-- at least we surely had a few dollars in our pocket. In the meantime, somehow or other, two brothers by the name of the Prisent found out about us. They were also from Torun. And the older one was a tutor of mine that, when I went that to Yeshiva, and he came Torun, to us and we were surprised that he was able to find this. How did you know that we-- he said, I heard that you're trying to go back to Torun. Would you mind if we go with you? At the spur of the moment, I wasn't too happy about this proposition because I was afraid the bigger the crowd, it's more suspicious. It's dangerous. But we couldn't refuse. We couldn't say no to them. So I said, yes, we are coming tomorrow. We are going tomorrow morning. We made up. Next morning, the one brother came to run and he said, my younger brother, Jank can't come. I don't remember the reason why. But he will be able to come at noontime. Could we postpone to noontime? All right, so do we have an answer? We will postpone to noontime. The reason, I don't remember what he said. Anyway, we waited the next day to noontime where all of the sudden they came, and we are ready to go on our way.

speaker: interviewee
text: We made out also that we will be 50, 100 meters apart, single from each other, and that those are rolling hills there on both sides of the highway. And we decided we will leave this afternoon. But then we knew that we will not be able to make the 50 kilometers in the afternoon. We will have to stay somewhere overnight. So we left, and we figured we'll go to the middle between Dolina and the halfway was a little town called Wygoda. And we figured we'll get Wygoda, and we'll stay there overnight. And then we will go next day. And so it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to Wygoda. It was all ready starting getting dark, and we met some women, Ukrainian women, on the road And we asked them if there are-- we knew that there were Jewish people around in these villages. We asked them, are there some Jews in here? In Wygoda. So they said, no, there are no Jews. All the Jews left when the war broke out. They're all in Dolina. There are not any Jews here. Alongside the highway, we see a house. Right away, I look at the mezuzah. I see a mezuzah. The windows have broken. The door is not locked-- open. It's an abandoned house. I knew it was a Jewish house. So we moved in there, moved in this house at the hope that nobody saw us-- not a human. Like a cat and a dog moves into a place, but a cat and a dog is not afraid that another cat or another dog is going to come to kill it. We were afraid that who the hell knows, somebody saw us, is going to kill us. Maybe they feel that we have money or something. We got into this house, and some glasses were broken, and scattered on the floor were religious books, papers all over the place. And we found a corner, and we huddled up in this corner. The wind was howling because of the open glass that was missing in the house. The wind was making a lot of terrible noise. And in one room, we found some straw. So we took some of that straw, and we spread it out. And this was the worst thing that we did. We laid down on that straw, and it didn't take long, a few minutes, really. All of a sudden, everything started crawling all over us. It was just like rain coming down. We realized, oh my god, what do we have? We were itching all-- So we tried to find another corner. We left the straw. We tried to find another corner. And here, I'm going to tell you-- how many minutes do we have? Here I'm going to tell you the biggest miracle of all happened, and we saw it that day. I wish I would know the day, but I don't. That was the luckiest day on the return. We started as soon as dawn broke. We started out walking, and we knew we have 25, 30 kilometers, which would be three, four hours. By 8 o'clock, 9 o'clock, 10 o'clock, we will be in Wyszkow. We will be seven, eight kilometers only just to get across.

speaker: interviewee
text: We made up-- the same way, the way we walk-- we made up that, in case we see somebody, we are to get off the highway. All of a sudden, just in the dark, the dawn-- it broke. All a sudden, I see two German soldiers with the bayonets, a patrol, coming against us. And here I am with my brother Tsvi, and the two brothers Prisent are 100 meters behind us. Because of the dark, we didn't see them from a distance. We were right on top of them, and there was no way we could get out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we started marching straight, straight, and we came close. And my hair started standing up. And my older brother Tsvi said to them, guten morgen, good morning. The two soldiers answered, good morning. We went that way, they went that way. I just looked at my brother Tsvi, and I say, what happened here that a patrol-- we just passed a patrol and patrol did not stop us? We had the white armbands on. I can't believe that this happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: We started to increase this pace of walking. Then we were finally were daring to look back, and I see that the two Prisent brothers also passed. They didn't stop them either. We passed by. We got off the road, just to catch our breath. We sat down a kilometer, and I remember that my brother Tsvi said to me, he said, we will be lucky. We will be successful to cross the border. Our sages say that no person recognizes a miracle when it happens to him. And in Hebrew it says, [HEBREW]. You don't know. You don't recognize when it happens to you. But it happened then and we did recognize it. It was an absolute miracle. I don't know. They did not stop us. And I guess we are close.

speaker: interviewer
text: You still have 10 minutes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Still have 10 minutes?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your brother, Moshe?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was left in Dolina. We left him in Dolina. So we pass there, and we walked and it took for another hour or two, and then we could see from-- it got daylight. We could see from the daylight the Carpathian mountain, the crest. It's all pine forest, covered. It was covered. I understand, it's denuded now. We came there. We saw from far away a farmer, a house, with a barn. There were cattle outside, and they were grazing. They were grazing, so we decided we will go into this place.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we got into this place, and we found an old couple, a Ukrainian couple. And we asked them if they would, for money, if they would show us the way how to cross-- which way to cross the border. So the old man said that he doesn't do that, but his son is coming home later and his son will do that. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] But he wants 50 pengos for that. Here we have only 40 pengos we have. And the two Prisent brothers that were with us, they didn't even have a one nickel with them. So we started to hassle with her. We said, we only have 30 pengos. That's all we have that we can give you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then he noticed that my brother has a watch on his hand. He says, well, you give us the 30 pengos, and your brother will give us the watch, my son will take you over. So we realized that this was already not the first time that he is doing it. He has already a price. We are not the first ones that passed over there. And meantime, he didn't want to-- yes, I have to mention. She, the woman, cooked up a porridge, oatmeal. Oatmeal with a little milk and that was the best oatmeal I ever ate because we were already hungry, going a couple of days without anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he told us to get out of the house. He didn't want us in the house. He was afraid. But he had a barn, he had cattle, to get in there. He said his son is going to come this afternoon. His son came and the price was already settled with old man. And the young man took us took us to the door. And he says, come over here, and I'll show you. You look at this mountain, there. And he said, in Ukrainian, he said, you see this [UKRAINIAN] over there? And he used the word [UKRAINIAN] which was a little bit strange to us. We didn't catch on the word. And [UKRAINIAN] in Ukrainian, means an open space inside a forest. They call that [UKRAINIAN]. He said, do you see that on top of that mountain? The [UKRAINIAN]? He said, when it's going to get dark, around 4 o'clock, 5 o'clock-- in the end of October, it's getting dark already that time-- he said, you try to get up to this [UKRAINIAN] and at the edge of that place, you'll stay there. And I'll come, and it'll be about 4:30 or 4 o'clock. I don't remember exactly. I'll be there, and from there I'm going to take you and show you which way to cross the border.

speaker: interviewee
text: It got dark, and we got out. And I remember now, we started running till we get to this [UKRAINIAN]. But fortunately, we tried to get lost among the trees, because the trees came down only to a certain place and the other place was empty. So we were running toward-- which was not very wise to do because this, in itself, made us suspicious, but that's the way we did it. And we went into this here-- once we got lost in the forest, we came to the [UKRAINIAN] and when we got there, we all of a sudden hear voices.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got scared. What voices? We heard talking. So it turned out we had a little scare that that was shepherds that were gathering-- they had sheep on that [UKRAINIAN]. And they were gathering them up, and they were talking to themselves. So they didn't see us, and they got away. And we waited for them. Here we had-- we still had that one watch. And here we looked at the watch, and he was a little bit late, and my brother Tsvi said, I think if he doesn't come I know how to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know we're going to the right there, and we will come over on the other side. And those are the [NON-ENGLISH] there. The other side of it was already the former Czechoslovakia. He knew it. I was never that close there. And also, this other guy, Prisent, he said, he also-- he agreed that this is-- well, what turned out to be was this guy came. He came late, but he came. We had to pay him right away, and he started getting quick, quick. He started climbing this mountain. We climbed the mountain, and he is climbing just the opposite way. And I remember my brother saying to me, he is taking us the wrong way. He is going to take us right into the border guards. But he was climbing, and there was no time to argue him. You had to follow him. We followed him. He came about 150 meters or 200 meters below the crest, and he says, [UKRAINIAN]. I'm not going any further. The crest here, that's the border there, and you cross right over there. And you'll see you will be on the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] in the fields. And he said good-bye, and he left right away. All of a sudden, we heard dogs barking. [SIGHS] And this was another miracle that happened. The Hungarians were patrolling the border on the crest-- that's where the-- [GESTURES] of the mountain. They were patrolling it. And while we heard the dogs, the dogs got scent of us, most likely, and they were wild. And we laid down, quiet as we were-- barely, barely breathing. And we hear the dogs louder and louder. And we hear already the patrol talking to themselves in Hungarian. That's how close we were. And the dogs. We were without breath, stopped breathing. But they passed. They passed, and the dogs did not stop barking. The fact that they didn't stop barking, we could ascertain how far they are away. And we waited maybe for another 10, 15 minutes, and the echo came in further-- weaker, weaker. And we realized that this patrol just passed by here and they are a whole distance away, and then we took a dash right across the. border.
